macro_segments.title: Astronomical Visibility of the New Moon and the Requirement that Both Day and Night Belong to the Month

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the requirement that both night and day of Rosh Chodesh must be from the new month
Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish: Two verses establishing that a day encompasses night and day together
Abaye and Rava on the difference between the two sources: the issue of chatzos (midnight)
Rashi's explanation: the requirement prevents Beis Din from obviously manipulating the calendar by declaring a day Rosh Chodesh when the old moon was seen the night before
Rabbeinu Chananel's interpretation: the entire day of Rosh Chodesh must fall after the molad (lunar conjunction), not during the old moon phase
Detailed analysis of when the molad occurs relative to moon visibility: the six-hour rule and the practical eighteen-hour delay
Scenario analysis: whether the entire day or only post-chatzos must be post-molad, connecting to the eating of matzah
Modern fixed calendar practice: why the molad is announced for Jerusalem time and the day after

macro_segments.title: Uncertainty About Rosh Chodesh in Distant Lands (Bavel) and the Principle of Sefeika DeYoma

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's principle: any doubt about Rosh Chodesh goes forward to the next day, so we only worry about months being 30 days, not 29
Why we don't worry about earlier uncertainties: multiple consecutive short months would eventually be discovered
Application to the 15th and 16th of festivals: keeping both days due to uncertainty
The story of Levi in Bavel: he encounters Jews eating on what he knows is Yom Kippur in Eretz Yisrael but refuses to testify without formal declaration
Tosafot's explanation: even without 100% certainty, communities identified a particular day as Rosh Hashanah, making it binding locally

macro_segments.title: The Geographic Reach of Messengers and Two-Day Observance in Different Locations

micro_segments.title: Rav Yochanan's proclamation: different rules for areas reached by Nissan messengers but not Tishrei messengers
Geographic map: Eretz Yisrael at center, zones of messenger reach, and communities that know in time for Pesach but not Succos
Gzeirah Nissan atu Tishrei: uniformity requirement that drives two-day observance in the gap zone
Story of Rabbi Aibo bar Nagri and Rabbi Chiyya bar Abba: they tolerated one-day Pesach in the gap zone; Rav Yochanan rebuked them

macro_segments.title: Two-Day Observance of Yom Kippur and the Uncertainty of Month-Length

micro_segments.title: Rava's practice of observing two days of Yom Kippur due to the uncertainty whether Elul is 29 or 30 days
Rava is vindicated when Elul turns out to be 30 days: demonstrating the risk of miscalculation
Rabbeinu Chananel: this is merely stringency, not an obligation, since Elul should always be 29 days
Story of Rav Nachman fasting Yom Kippur: a messenger arrives at Ne'ilah to report that Elul was 30 days and tomorrow is Yom Kippur
Rav Nachman's anger and wordplay curse; the challenge of fasting two consecutive days

macro_segments.title: The Solar Season and the Requirement to Add a Leap Month

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna bar Avin's teaching to Rava: if the winter solstice extends into the 16th of Nisan, add a leap month so Pesach falls in spring
Verse basis: 'Shamor et chodesh ha-aviv'—both the lunar month (chodesh) and solar season (aviv) must align
Rashi: the month must be 'chadesh' (still waxing), so spring must begin by the 14th or earlier, requiring a full leap month rather than one-day adjustment
Limitation on calendar adjustments: cannot exceed certain imbalances like 8 and 4 in month distribution

macro_segments.title: Lunar Phase Markers for Calculating the Middle of the Month at Sea

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's instruction to seafarers: when the moon completes the day (sets as sun rises), destroy chametz because Pesach is approaching
Explanation of the astronomical marker: on the 15th of the month, sun and moon are diametrically opposite; moon visible all night and sets at sunrise
For those on land with clear horizons, the marker shifts to the 14th due to enhanced visibility relative to those at sea level

macro_segments.title: Violation of Shabbat for Testimony about Rosh Chodesh: Nissan and Tishrei

micro_segments.title: Mishna: witnesses can violate Shabbat to testify about the new moon only for Nissan and Tishrei
Reason: only these months have festivals dependent on correct dating; they are the hinge upon which all Yamim Tovim depend
Gemara resolves apparent contradiction: messengers go out for all months, but only at night (post facto); for Nissan and Tishrei they await formal declaration before leaving
Verse derivation: 'Eleh moadei Hashem asher tikre'u otam be-mo'adam'—declare them in their right time overrides Shabbat
Post-Destruction: Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai limited Shabbat violation to Nissan and Tishrei only, since other months no longer have korban obligations
Interpretation: Yochanan ben Zakkai's takannot respond to post-Mikdash reality rather than attempting to preserve pre-Destruction practice

topical_tags.term: Layla v'Yom Min HaChodesh (night and day must be from the month)
Bavel (Babylonia) - distant lands without timely messenger service
Rav Yochanan's ruling on two-day observance
Gzeirah Nissan atu Tishrei (uniform observance of Pesach and Succos)
Levi's refusal to testify about Yom Kippur
Elul - month preceding Tishrei; standard 29 days
Tekufas Teves (winter solstice) and Aviv (spring season)
Violation of Shabbat for Rosh Chodesh testimony
Nissan and Tishrei - critical months for festivals
Beis Din (court) authority to declare Rosh Chodesh
Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai's post-Destruction takannot
